metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `attempts=1` to the claim-primitive lock call spec to match the txn pattern exactly: `lock.acquire(tracker, timeout=30, attempts=1, dual_window=True)`.
findings.finding: The plan's claim-primitive spec says to use `lock.acquire(tracker, timeout=30, dual_window=True)` mirroring 'the txn pattern', but `txn.py::_acquire_write_lock` explicitly passes `attempts=1` to keep the budget at 30s. The bare `lock.acquire` defaults to `attempts=2` (a 60s budget). Without specifying `attempts=1`, the drainer's claim lock would silently use a 60s budget instead of the intended 30s.
findings.location: Atomicity + claim section: 'lock.acquire(tracker, timeout=30, dual_window=True) -> LockHandle'
findings.suggested_fix: Update the Atomicity + claim section: 'lock.acquire(tracker, timeout=30, **attempts=1**, dual_window=True) -> LockHandle' with a note that attempts=1 matches txn.py's 30s budget.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] In the 'Race-free claim' section, explicitly state that the CLAIM_ENRICH append inside the held `LockHandle` must be done **inline** (write file + `git add` + `git commit` directly, as txn.py does), NOT via `stage_and_commit`/`write_and_push`, which re-acquire the lock internally and will fail with `LockTimeout` under a held `LockHandle`.
findings.finding: The plan specifies that CLAIM_ENRICH is appended inside a manually held `lock.acquire` critical section (following the txn.py pattern), but does not explicitly state that the append must be done inline (manual write + git-add + commit) rather than via `stage_and_commit`/`write_and_push`. Those helpers re-acquire the write lock internally via `lock.write_lock`; with the outer `LockHandle` already holding the fcntl fd (LOCK_NB), the inner fcntl attempt fails immediately with an OSError (non-blocking), causing the CLAIM_ENRICH append to fail with a LockTimeout. An implementer reading only the CLAIM section could naturally reach for the standard `stage_and_commit` API and produce silently broken claim logic.
findings.location: ## Atomicity + claim — 'Race-free claim' paragraph; 'Scope (exact edit targets)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the 'Race-free claim' description: 'The CLAIM_ENRICH append inside the held LockHandle must follow the txn.py inline pattern (fsutil.atomic_write → git add → git commit), never stage_and_commit/write_and_push, which call lock.write_lock internally and will immediately fail with LockTimeout while the outer LockHandle is held (fcntl is LOCK_NB).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the Testing section how `test_claim_race` exercises concurrency (e.g., two threads synchronized at a `threading.Barrier` before the lock-acquire, or a mock that lets both readers proceed before either appends) so the fixture genuinely exercises the race window.
findings.finding: The `test_claim_race` proving command does not specify how concurrency is exercised (two threads, two sequential calls against shared mocked state, two processes, etc.), creating a fixture-invalidity risk: an executor could write a purely sequential test that always passes without ever testing the race condition.
findings.location: Testing section: `test_claim_race (two claimers in one critical section → exactly one CLAIM lands)` and AC3
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the test description: 'Use threading.Barrier(2) to release both claimers simultaneously against a single shared mock store; assert exactly one CLAIM_ENRICH in the event log after both threads complete.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the reducer definition how CLAIM_ENRICH and DONE_ENRICH are matched to a specific ENQUEUE_ENRICH version (e.g., include an enqueue sequence/HLC in CLAIM and DONE payloads, and require the reducer to check that DONE post-dates the latest ENQUEUE), so a re-cert after a claim correctly surfaces the new entry as pending.
findings.finding: The reducer spec does not define whether a CLAIM_ENRICH appended before the latest ENQUEUE_ENRICH (i.e., after a re-cert supersedes the earlier enqueue) is still treated as a valid claim on the new entry, or whether the new ENQUEUE supersedes/invalidates the prior claim. This is a live lifecycle ambiguity: a drainer that claimed under the old enqueue may complete enrichment on stale content, then DONE_ENRICH (emitted by S4) would mark the new (re-cert) queue entry as done without ever having been enriched against the new cert.
findings.location: '## What' reducer definition and 'Atomicity + claim' section
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `enqueue_hlc` field to both CLAIM_ENRICH and DONE_ENRICH payloads that records the HLC of the ENQUEUE_ENRICH being acted on. The reducer then treats a DONE as closing only the matching enqueue version; a CLAIM whose enqueue_hlc < latest ENQUEUE HLC is treated as stale/unclaimed.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify which clock value is 'cert' in `not_before_ns = cert + SOAK`: state explicitly whether it is the SIGNATURE event's `signed_at` (wall-clock `time.time_ns()`) or `hlc.next_tick()` at enqueue time, and update the HLC/wall-clock instruction accordingly.
findings.finding: The plan specifies `not_before_ns = cert + SOAK` and says 'using the HLC clock for not_before_ns', but `cert`'s timestamp — the value returned by `sign_plan_review` — is wall-clock (`time.time_ns()` via `signing.sign_manifest`), not an HLC tick. The plan does not resolve which clock 'cert' means in this arithmetic.
findings.location: Scope section — 'Enqueue hook + the atomic cert-critical-section in src/rebar/llm/plan_review/ certification path, using lock.acquire (the txn pattern) and the HLC clock for not_before_ns'
findings.suggested_fix: Use the wall-clock `time.time_ns()` at enqueue time (consistent with `signed_at` being wall-clock, per attest.py line 551) for the `cert` base, and drop the 'HLC clock' qualifier from `not_before_ns`. The HLC is still used for the ENQUEUE_ENRICH event's filename/ordering timestamp; only the `not_before_ns` data field uses wall-clock.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace 'like REVIEW_RESULT/TICKET_DIGEST' with 'like REVIEW_RESULT' (the only confirmed existing precedent) and note that TICKET_DIGEST will be added by S2 independently; verify the frozenset edit in this story does not conflict with S2's edit.
findings.finding: The plan cites 'like REVIEW_RESULT/TICKET_DIGEST' as the concrete precedent for adding the three new types to `_NON_REPLAY_KNOWN_TYPES`, but TICKET_DIGEST does not yet exist in `_NON_REPLAY_KNOWN_TYPES` (or anywhere in the codebase) — S2 (wispy-cheerless-tick) is still open/unmerged. Using an unmerged type as a parity model is a false codebase-state assertion.
findings.location: Scope section: 'src/rebar/reducer/_version.py: add to _NON_REPLAY_KNOWN_TYPES (sidecar; like REVIEW_RESULT/TICKET_DIGEST)'
findings.suggested_fix: Change the scope note to: 'add to `_NON_REPLAY_KNOWN_TYPES` (sidecar; like REVIEW_RESULT — TICKET_DIGEST will be added by S2 independently in the same frozenset)' and flag the two-story edit as a merge-ordering concern.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit cardinality assertion to `test_claim_race`: assert the event log contains EXACTLY one `CLAIM_ENRICH` for the contested ticket_id after both claimers complete (e.g., `assert sum(1 for e in store.events if e.type == 'CLAIM_ENRICH' and e.ticket_id == tid) == 1`).
findings.finding: AC3's cardinality claim ('exactly one CLAIM_ENRICH lands') lacks an explicit assertion on event count; the proving command `pytest ...::test_claim_race` can satisfy the AC even if the test only checks that at least one CLAIM_ENRICH exists (not that there are no duplicates).
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC3: 'Two concurrent claimers → exactly one CLAIM_ENRICH lands'
findings.suggested_fix: In the test description write: 'assert `len([e for e in store.events if e.type == "CLAIM_ENRICH" and e.ticket_id == tid]) == 1` — not merely that one exists.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the error-handling contract for the post-certification ENQUEUE_ENRICH append: at minimum, log a warning with ticket_id and exception on failure so operators can detect enqueue misses without relying solely on the S4 fallback scan.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify error handling for the case where the ENQUEUE_ENRICH append (the separate post-certification append) itself raises an exception — whether it is surfaced to the caller, logged, or silently swallowed.
findings.location: ## Atomicity + claim — 'Enqueue placement (no false atomicity)' paragraph; ## Acceptance Criteria — AC 1
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap the post-cert append in a try/except; on failure emit a structured log warning (level=WARNING, fields: ticket_id, error, fallback='s4_scan') and optionally increment a counter metric — do not re-raise (cert already succeeded), but do not silently swallow either.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the plan whether `not_before_ns` is computed as `time.time_ns() + SOAK_NS` (physical clock, always correct for wall-time soak) or `hlc.next_tick() + SOAK_NS` (ordering clock, which can exceed wall-clock by a witnessed-skew delta), and confirm the drain's `now_ns` uses the same domain.
findings.finding: The plan states `not_before_ns = cert + SOAK` and describes using 'the HLC clock for `not_before_ns`', but does not resolve whether `cert` (an HLC tick, which is `max(cache, witness, physical_now()) + 1`) is semantically interchangeable with a wall-clock nanosecond timestamp for the purposes of adding a 60-minute physical-time soak and then comparing against `time.time_ns()` at drain time. If HLC ever runs significantly ahead of wall-clock (legitimate per the spec: it witnesses other events' prefixes), `cert + SOAK` could set `not_before_ns` further into the future than intended, extending the soak silently.
findings.location: ## What — `ENQUEUE_ENRICH {ticket_id, not_before_ns}` / Enqueue hook in `plan_review/` certification path using HLC clock
findings.suggested_fix: Compute `not_before_ns = time.time_ns() + SOAK_NS` (physical clock) regardless of what HLC tick is used for the event filename timestamp. The HLC tick is the event ordering key; the soak deadline is a wall-clock comparison. They are semantically different and should use different clock sources.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact module path for `pending_enrichment` (e.g. `src/rebar/llm/overlap/enrich_queue.py` or `src/rebar/reducer/enrich_queue.py`) in the Scope section so S4's drain can import it without guessing.
findings.finding: The `pending_enrichment` reducer has no specified module path. The plan names the function but never says which file it lives in — it is not in any existing reducer file, not in an existing llm/plan_review file, and no new file is named for it.
findings.location: ## What — 'Reducer `pending_enrichment(now_ns)`' / ## Scope (exact edit targets)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Scope: '`src/rebar/llm/overlap/enrich_queue.py` (new): `pending_enrichment(tracker, now_ns)` reducer + the claim primitive (imports from `rebar._store.lock` directly).' This mirrors the REVIEW_RESULT sidecar pattern where sidecar logic lives next to its consumer in `llm/plan_review/sidecar.py`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add the claim primitive's function name and module path to the Scope section (e.g. `claim_enrich(tracker, ticket_id, drainer_id, lease_ttl_ns)` in `src/rebar/llm/overlap/enrich_queue.py`) so S4 can import it by a stable, reviewed path.
findings.finding: The claim primitive — the 'read-reduce-check-then-append inside a single held lock' function — has no named home file or symbol. The plan describes the algorithm but neither names the function nor specifies which module exposes it for use by S4's drain.
findings.location: ## Atomicity + claim — 'Race-free claim' / ## Scope (exact edit targets)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define `drainer_id` value vocabulary in the CLAIM_ENRICH schema (e.g., `f"{socket.gethostname()}:{os.getpid()}"` mirroring lock.py's `_owner_stamp`) so implementers and test assertions agree on the identity format.
findings.finding: `drainer_id` appears in the CLAIM_ENRICH payload schema but its value vocabulary (how it is generated — UUID, hostname:pid, process ID, etc.) is never defined anywhere in the plan.
findings.location: What section — `CLAIM_ENRICH {ticket_id, drainer_id, lease_expires_ns}`
findings.suggested_fix: Adopt the existing `_owner_stamp()` pattern from `src/rebar/_store/lock.py` (line 143: `f"{socket.gethostname()}:{os.getpid()}"`), which is already the proven per-process identity for the same store. State this explicitly in the CLAIM_ENRICH schema description.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the error-handling policy for a failed ENQUEUE_ENRICH append: state whether it is best-effort (log + continue, cert succeeds regardless, S4 self-healing recovers) or propagating, mirroring the explicit best-effort stance used by REVIEW_RESULT sidecar.
findings.finding: The failure mode of the ENQUEUE_ENRICH append (StoreError, LockTimeout, RebaseGuard from `append_event`) is unspecified — the plan does not state whether a failed ENQUEUE should be best-effort (log and continue, cert still succeeds) or propagate as an error to the caller.
findings.location: Atomicity + claim section — 'ENQUEUE_ENRICH is a SEPARATE append immediately after sign_plan_review returns'
findings.suggested_fix: State explicitly: 'ENQUEUE_ENRICH is best-effort — a StoreError/LockTimeout/RebaseGuard is logged as a warning and does not fail the certification; the S4 self-healing scan is the recovery path (same stance as the REVIEW_RESULT sidecar).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add the file path for `pending_enrichment(now_ns)` to the Scope section (e.g., `src/rebar/llm/overlap/queue.py` or `src/rebar/llm/overlap/__init__.py`) so S4 has an unambiguous import target.
findings.finding: The `pending_enrichment(now_ns)` reducer function's file location is never specified — the plan names the function and its contract but does not place it in any file path, leaving implementers to guess whether it lives in src/rebar/reducer/, src/rebar/llm/overlap/, or elsewhere.
findings.location: What section — 'Reducer pending_enrichment(now_ns) → entries past soak, unclaimed-or-lease-expired, with no later DONE' and Scope section

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the Scope section's 'using `lock.acquire` (the txn pattern)' phrase with the Atomicity section's explicit statement that enqueue does NOT use the held lock; the Scope description should match the Atomicity section (i.e., plain `append_event` after `sign_plan_review` returns, no compound lock).
findings.finding: The Scope section says the enqueue hook uses 'lock.acquire (the txn pattern)', but the Atomicity section explicitly states the enqueue does NOT use the held lock — it is a separate append immediately after `sign_plan_review` returns, relying on `append_event`'s own per-event lock. These two sections directly contradict each other on whether the enqueue holds a compound lock.
findings.location: Scope section ('Enqueue hook + the atomic cert-critical-section … using `lock.acquire` (the txn pattern)') vs. Atomicity section ('the enqueue does NOT')
findings.suggested_fix: In Scope, change 'Enqueue hook + the atomic cert-critical-section in src/rebar/llm/plan_review/ certification path, using lock.acquire (the txn pattern)' to 'Enqueue hook in src/rebar/llm/plan_review/ certification path (plain append_event call immediately after sign_plan_review returns; no held compound lock — see Atomicity section); claim primitive uses lock.acquire (the txn pattern).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion that verifies `overlap_soak_min` and `overlap_lease_ttl_min` exist on `LLMConfig` with correct defaults (60 and 15 min respectively) and that `not_before_ns` / `lease_expires_ns` values in the enqueue and claim tests are derived from config rather than hard-coded.
findings.finding: The Scope section describes two config deliverables — `overlap_soak_min` (60) and `overlap_lease_ttl_min` (15) on `LLMConfig` — but no acceptance criterion verifies their existence, default values, or env-var/TOML wiring. They are uncovered deliverables.
findings.location: Scope section: 'Config field target src/rebar/llm/config.py: overlap_soak_min (60), overlap_lease_ttl_min (15)' vs. Acceptance Criteria (all five bullets)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a rollback note to the plan asserting the post-rollback schema state: that existing ENQUEUE_ENRICH/CLAIM_ENRICH/DONE_ENRICH event files remain on disk after a code rollback and will trigger `is_unknown_newer_type()` fsck warnings on old binaries, and document the back-out options (re-deploy, or accept warnings as harmless given append-only no-data-loss semantics).
findings.finding: The plan does not assert on the schema state after rollback: if the three new event types (ENQUEUE_ENRICH, CLAIM_ENRICH, DONE_ENRICH) have been appended and the code is then rolled back, those events remain on disk but are absent from the rolled-back binary's _NON_REPLAY_KNOWN_TYPES, causing is_unknown_newer_type() to return True for them and fsck to warn about 'newer than me' events on every run. No back-out procedure (e.g., note that rolled-back code will emit fsck warnings until the new event files are removed or the code is re-deployed) is specified.
findings.location: Scope section (event_append.py / reducer/_version.py edit targets); AC bullet 5 ('The three event types are in EVENT_TYPES + _NON_REPLAY_KNOWN_TYPES')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Rollback / forward-compat' note to the Scope or Atomicity section: 'After rollback, already-appended ENQUEUE_ENRICH/CLAIM_ENRICH/DONE_ENRICH events remain on disk. A rolled-back binary's _NON_REPLAY_KNOWN_TYPES does not include these types, so fsck warns about them as unknown-newer-type events. This is cosmetically noisy but causes no data loss (append-only store). Back-out: re-deploy the feature, or (if a clean store is required) remove the new event files from git history — but the latter is unnecessary in practice since the events are harmless sidecar files.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the title/body conflict: rename 'optimistic claim' in the title to 'lock-guarded claim' (or 'mutex-claim') to match the body's explicit `lock.acquire` critical-section description.
findings.finding: The plan title says 'optimistic claim' while the body consistently describes a lock-guarded, read-reduce-check-append pattern inside a held manual lock — the two terms carry conflicting mental models (optimistic = no lock, lock-guarded = explicit mutex).
findings.location: Title: 'optimistic claim' vs. body: 'Atomicity + claim' section — 'read-reduce-check-then-append inside a single held lock.acquire critical section'
findings.suggested_fix: Change the title suffix from 'optimistic claim' to 'lock-guarded claim' so it matches the body: 'cert-triggered enqueue, 60-min soak, latest-wins, lock-guarded claim'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a deliverable (ADR, CLAUDE.md section, or design-doc note) capturing the decision to model the enrichment queue as sidecar events rather than a separate broker/store, including the tradeoffs acknowledged in the 'Why' section.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel architectural decision (event-sourced sidecar queue as async enrichment substrate) but does not specify that an ADR, CLAUDE.md entry, or design-doc update will be produced to capture the decision rationale and tradeoffs.
findings.location: ## Why / ## What / ## Scope sections
findings.suggested_fix: Add a single ADR or a paragraph to CLAUDE.md under a 'Design Decisions' heading covering: (1) why sidecar events over SQLite/broker, (2) the latest-wins reduce semantics, (3) the non-atomic enqueue + self-healing recovery contract with S4.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least structured logging (or a counter metric) for: ENQUEUE_ENRICH appended, CLAIM_ENRICH won/lost, lease-expired reclaim, and lock-acquire timeout in the claim path — and include an observability AC or scope line.
findings.finding: No observability (metrics, logs, or traces) is specified for any of the new failure/state-transition points: enqueue, claim race loss, lease expiry, or lock timeout during claim.
findings.location: ## Testing; ## Acceptance Criteria (no observability AC present)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the document title with the implementation: either rename the title to 'lock-guarded claim' (matching H1 and the body) or, if optimistic/CAS-style claim is truly intended, revise the Atomicity+claim section to match.
findings.finding: The document title says 'optimistic claim' but the H1 heading and entire implementation section describe a pessimistic, lock-guarded claim (manual lock.acquire critical section). An implementer reading only the title — or a downstream spec referencing this story by title — will expect optimistic concurrency (CAS/version-check, no global lock) and may be confused or implement differently.
findings.location: Document title vs. H1 heading and 'Atomicity + claim' section
findings.suggested_fix: Change the document title to match H1: 'Event-sourced enrichment queue: cert-triggered enqueue, 60-min soak, latest-wins, lock-guarded claim'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether `overlap_soak_min` and `overlap_lease_ttl_min` need to be operator-tunable config keys in v1 (vs. module-level constants like `RETAIN_PER_TICKET = 10` in sidecar.py), given these values have no production tuning history and S4 adds three more `REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_*` config keys in the same epic.
findings.finding: overlap_soak_min and overlap_lease_ttl_min are proposed as LLMConfig fields (with REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_SOAK_MIN / REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_LEASE_TTL_MIN env vars and [tool.rebar.llm] file keys) from day one, but these two constants have no production tuning history and could start as module-level constants (matching the RETAIN_PER_TICKET=10 pattern in plan_review/sidecar.py), being promoted to config only when operators demonstrate a need to tune them. Combined with S4's three overlap_drain/overlap_drain_batch/overlap_drain_gate_budget_ms fields, the overlap feature introduces 5 new config-surface entries across two stories, none with an existing operational baseline.
findings.location: Scope section / Config field target: `overlap_soak_min` (60), `overlap_lease_ttl_min` (15) on `LLMConfig`
findings.suggested_fix: Start `SOAK = 60 * 60 * 10**9` and `LEASE_TTL = 15 * 60 * 10**9` as module-level constants in the new queue module (matching the `RETAIN_PER_TICKET` pattern). Promote to LLMConfig fields in a follow-up story only if operators request tuning.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Make explicit that the self-healing fallback (digest-absent/stale → re-enqueue) is BLOCKED on S2 (wispy-cheerless-tick) and cannot be implemented until S2's `latest_ticket_digest` / freshness API is merged; add S2 as a hard dependency for the drain's self-healing path.
findings.finding: The plan's self-healing fallback relies on S2 (wispy-cheerless-tick, the TICKET_DIGEST story) providing a 'digest freshness' check: 'treats any certified ticket with an absent/stale digest as enqueable'. But S2 is open/unmerged, so the digest sidecar and its freshness API (`latest_ticket_digest`, freshness check) do not yet exist. The plan's fallback correctness guarantee and the drain's skip-decision logic depend on S2's output existing at runtime.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria AC1 and Atomicity + claim section: 'self-healing fallback treats any certified ticket with an absent/stale digest as enqueable'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/__init__.py` (at the `sign_plan_review` call site, line ~482) as the explicit edit target for the enqueue hook, and clarify whether the ENQUEUE_ENRICH append is inside or outside the existing signing try-except block.
findings.finding: The enqueue hook call-site file within `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/` is not named. `sign_plan_review` is called from `__init__.py:482` inside a try-except block; the ENQUEUE_ENRICH append must follow the signing call, but the plan only names the directory, not the file.
findings.location: ## Scope — 'Enqueue hook + the atomic cert-critical-section in `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/`'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm the StoreError error-message string at `event_append.py:98-103` is updated to include ENQUEUE_ENRICH, CLAIM_ENRICH, DONE_ENRICH alongside the frozenset addition (both sites must change atomically).
findings.finding: The `StoreError` message at `event_append.py:98-103` is a hardcoded concatenated string that enumerates every valid type by name. Adding three new types requires editing this string — but the plan's phrasing '(+ StoreError message)' understates that the string also has a structural constraint (it must stay in sync with EVENT_TYPES) and no AC or test pins the message content.
findings.location: ## Scope — '`src/rebar/_store/event_append.py` `EVENT_TYPES` (+ StoreError message)'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
